steps.title: Identify what the passage establishes
steps.content: The passage argues that the Aubrey/Maturin novels have abrupt endings that connect to the next book, and concludes the series is best understood as a single, incredibly long work. The correct answer must reflect this structural argument.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — It is unfair that the Aubrey/Maturin series does not have the critical reputation of *The Forsyte Saga*, which has a similar structure.: Introduces a fairness judgment about critical reputation. The passage does not make this claim. ✗
  • Choice B — *The Far Side of the World and The Reverse of the Medal* are [two] of the most complex of Patrick O'Brian's [novels of the Aubrey/Maturin series] with unconventional structures.: Focuses narrowly on two specific books as the most complex; the passage is about the whole series' structure, not ranking individual novels. ✗
  • Choice C — The structure of each of the novels of the Aubrey/Maturin series suggests that the series should be considered a single continuous story.: Directly matches the passage's conclusion that abrupt, connected endings make the series one long continuous work. ✓
  • Choice D — Many readers find the Aubrey/Maturin novels to be remarkably entertaining despite flaws novels' structures.: Introduces reader entertainment and structural flaws; neither is discussed in the passage. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
